llm_extracted.ai_arm_selection_rationale: This paper does not report a controlled experiment comparing human-only vs. human+AI performance on a speed or productivity outcome. It is a descriptive/observational study of India's startup ecosystem (2016-2025), examining structural differences between pre-AI-era (2016-2020) and AI-era (2021-2025) cohorts. The study uses founding year as a natural experiment proxy, but does NOT measure task completion time, throughput, or any individual-level productivity metric. Instead, it reports firm-level metrics: employee counts, valuation per employee, revenue per employee, and funding ratios. These are aggregate organizational outcomes, not measures of how quickly or efficiently individuals complete tasks with vs. without AI assistance. The paper's primary findings are that AI-era firms have LARGER employee counts and LOWER efficiency ratios (valuation/employee, revenue/funding), which the authors interpret as early-stage investment in AI that has not yet yielded proportional returns. There is no human baseline arm performing tasks without AI, and no AI-assisted arm performing the same tasks with AI. The 'treatment' is the era in which a startup was founded, not an intervention where the same task is performed with/without AI. Therefore, this paper does not fit the SPEED effect size framework, which requires a comparison of task performance (time or throughput) between human-only and human+AI conditions.
llm_extracted.notes: After careful re-reading, this paper is fundamentally incompatible with the SPEED meta-analysis framework. It studies firm-level structural outcomes (employee counts, valuations, funding) across startup cohorts founded in different eras, not individual or team task performance with/without AI. The 'natural experiment' is observational (founding year as proxy for AI availability), not a controlled comparison of the same task performed under AI vs. no-AI conditions. The reported statistics (t-tests, regressions) compare aggregate firm metrics between cohorts, not productivity/speed metrics. For example, the paper reports that AI-era firms have mean age-adjusted employee count of 11.641 vs. 4.961 for pre-AI (t=-12.891, p<0.001), and valuation per employee of 0.788 vs. 1.188 (t=2.044, p=0.041). These are not measures of how fast tasks are completed. The paper explicitly states findings reveal 'larger structures and lower efficiency in AI-era firms' and 'early-stage investments in technology that have yet to yield proportional returns.' This is a macro-level ecosystem study, not a micro-level productivity study. No speed or throughput outcome exists.
source_quotes: This study explores the structural and performance impacts of artificial intelligence (AI) adoption on India's knowledge-intensive startups, spanning information technology, financial technology, health technology, and educational technology, founded between 2016 and 2025.

Using a natural experiment framework with the founding year as an exogenous treatment proxy, it examines firm size, revenue productivity, valuation efficiency, and capital utilization across pre-AI and AI-era cohorts.
Findings reveal larger structures and lower efficiency in AI-era firms, supported by a dataset of 914 cleaned firms.

AI-era firms attract higher funding and achieve higher absolute valuations, their per-employee productivity and efficiency ratios are lower, potentially indicating early-stage investments in technology that have yet to yield proportional returns.

AI-era firms employed more individuals on an age-adjusted basis (mean = 11.641, standard deviation = 9.684) compared to pre-AI firms (mean = 4.961, standard deviation = 5.252), with a t-value of -12.891 and 912 degrees of freedom, yielding a p-value less than 0.001

Valuation per employee was lower in AI-era firms (mean = 0.788, standard deviation = 1.689) than in pre-AI firms (mean = 1.188, standard deviation = 2.622), with a t-value of 2.044 and 912 degrees of freedom, p = 0.041

The revenue-to-funding ratio also decreased for AI-era firms (mean = 1.530, standard deviation = 10.087) compared to pre-AI firms (mean = 8.941, standard deviation = 55.213), with a t-value of 1.893 and 912 degrees of freedom, p = 0.059 (marginal)
